Synthetic financial dialogs · extraction benchmark

High-level approach for generating household data, dialogs, and extraction metrics

This page summarizes the end-to-end methodology across data generation, dialog generation, extraction scoring, Train/holdout test split construction, and current benchmark results.

5,000
Generated across 11 explicit scenarios
Train dialogs

Used to debug and tune the pipeline
Holdout test dialogs

Held out from extractor/evaluator tuning

Overview

The benchmark is built as a controlled synthetic-data pipeline

Table of contents
  1. Synthetic household generation
  2. Dialog generation and realism checks
  3. Matching, scoring, and errors
  4. Train/holdout test split logic
  5. Train and holdout test results
  6. Quality controls at each stage
  7. Next steps
  8. Suggested and developed improvement

Generate households

Sample affluent US RIA-like households from explicit scenario priors, public-data anchors, and lifecycle constraints.

Generate dialogs

Create long advisor-client conversations grounded in each household profile, with evidence files for field-level traceability.

Extract and score

Run structured extraction from transcript text, match extracted records to dialog-grounded GT, and score field-level accuracy.

Validate holdout test

Evaluate a held-out test set that was not used while tuning the extractor or evaluator.

1. Data generation

Household data is scenario-driven and distributionally checked

The generator creates affluent US advisor-like households rather than generic population samples. Scenarios are explicit, weighted, and validated for coverage. Public-data anchors and curated priors drive income, assets, liabilities, household structure, risk tolerance, and lifecycle fields.

Coverage requirement

11 scenarios

Every household belongs to one named archetype. This avoids hidden rare cases and makes coverage auditable.

Affluent conditioning

RIA-like population

Income and wealth distributions are shifted upward and constrained by an affluent income floor.

Validation

0 rule violations

The generated dataset has no business-rule violations in the current artifact set.

Generation rules and cross-field constraints

The household generator is not just sampling independent columns. It encodes scenario-specific dependencies so that income, assets, liabilities, family composition, dates, and lifecycle fields move together.

Income and assets

  • Household income is sampled from a smooth lognormal model anchored to public Census median household income.
  • The population is affluent-conditioned around a 2x public-median income floor, using resampling rather than a hard visible cutoff.
  • Scenario income multipliers adjust specific archetypes: retired households have lower income, self-employed owners skew higher, and young or financially stressed households stay closer to the floor.
  • Investable assets are generated as a noisy multiple of income, then adjusted by scenario, so high assets generally correspond to higher income without becoming a rigid segment label.
  • Age and retirement proximity modify income and assets: working households tend to peak closer to retirement, while retired households have lower income but higher accumulated assets.
  • Asset mix is decomposed into retirement, cash, alternatives, brokerage-style assets, and property, with retired households receiving more retirement assets and young low-asset households receiving less alternatives exposure.

Debt, mortgage, and expenses

  • Mortgage presence is scenario-dependent; family-with-mortgage and financially-stressed scenarios force mortgage exposure.
  • Mortgage payment is sampled as a bounded share of monthly income, using scenario-specific beta-shaped ranges and a hard validation cap at 70% of income.
  • Mortgage burden and remaining term taper down near retirement, so older and retired households are less likely to carry high mortgage exposure.
  • Mortgage outstanding is derived from payment and remaining term, then constrained by property LTV and income-multiple caps.
  • Financially-stressed households force non-mortgage debt and use higher non-mortgage payment and outstanding-balance ranges.
  • Total monthly expenses are built from non-debt expenses plus required debt payments, with scenario-specific expense-to-income ranges.

Household composition and lifecycle

  • Each scenario has an age band and couple/children rule: young dual-income households are couples without kids; family-with-mortgage households are couples with kids; widowed and divorced households are single-adult scenarios.
  • Spouse or partner age is generated near the primary client's age, with minimum and maximum adult-age bounds.
  • Child count is generated only for scenarios that allow children, with child ages capped and youngest/oldest child dates ordered consistently.
  • Parent age at child birth must be at least the configured minimum age.
  • Move-in dates cannot imply the primary client moved in before adulthood.
  • Employment start dates cannot precede minimum working age and cannot fall in the future.

Marital status, income lines, and products

  • Marital status can be scenario-overridden: widowed, divorced, and secondly-wedded households receive compatible statuses.
  • Alimony is only generated for divorced or secondly-wedded-paying-alimony households; the validator flags any incompatible alimony/status combination.
  • Household income is split across one or two adults, with a special high-earner/low-earner split for that scenario.
  • Income lines are decomposed into realistic sources such as salary, bonus, business income, rental income, Social Security, pension income, and interest/dividends.
  • Social Security and pension income are age-gated so they do not appear for implausibly young clients.
  • Protection policies derive coverage and premium behavior from income and term rules, with coverage dates capped to avoid implausible ages.

Selected data-generation diagnostics

The following figures are a representative slice of the diagnostic plots produced by Stage 01. Together they illustrate how we check scenario coverage, marginal distributions, cross-field consistency, debt burden, lifecycle effects, and categorical priors.

2. Dialog generation

Dialogs are generated in stages and checked for grounding and realism

Dialog generation does not ask a model to produce an entire transcript in one shot. It builds a persona, an outline, phase-level dialog turns, and state updates, then optionally finalizes the transcript into a more natural conversation.

Grounded transcript construction

The dialog generator consumes the household financial profile and scenario context. It writes a transcript, JSON metadata, evidence items, validation metrics, and plain-text transcript files.

The dialog generation model is GPT via the OpenAI API, configured as `gpt-5.2` in the dialog-generation `.env`. GPT is used to create personas, outlines, phase-level turns, state updates, and final transcript polishing.

Evidence artifacts map profile fields to advisor/client excerpts. These evidence files later become dialog-grounded GT for extraction scoring.

DeepSeek realism gate

Dialog realism is checked with an LLM-as-a-Judge approach. The judge is DeepSeek, configured through the DeepSeek API model id `deepseek-chat`; according to the current DeepSeek API changelog, this maps to DeepSeek-V3.2 non-thinking mode.

A DeepSeek judge scores finalized transcripts on a 1 to 5 realism scale. The standard gate copies transcripts into a `realism_passed` subset when the realism score meets the configured threshold.

In the Train set, 316 dialogs are from the realism-passed subset. For the holdout test, the requested evaluation used 99 valid generated dialogs with judge artifacts retained.

Why this matters

The extraction benchmark is only meaningful if the dialogs sound like plausible financial-discovery conversations and if each scored field can be traced back to information present in the transcript. The realism gate helps with conversational quality; evidence-based grounded GT helps with fair scoring.

Dialog generation README DeepSeek judge prompt Extraction scoring details
3. Matching and error accounting

Metrics are computed after record matching and grounded-aware field scoring

The evaluator compares extracted JSON to dialog-grounded GT. It normalizes both sides, pairs records by content when IDs are unstable, then scores fields cell-by-cell.

Record matching

Primary keys are used when reliable. Otherwise, content-based pairing is used for people, income lines, assets, liabilities, and protection policies.

Field scoring

Numeric fields use 1% relative tolerance. Multichoice fields are compared as sets. String-like fields are compared case-insensitively after normalization.

Error types

Errors are split into missing extracted values, invented extra values, value mismatches, and record-pairing failures.

Scoring assumptions

Assumption Implementation Why it matters
Dialog-grounded denominator When `ground_truth_is_grounded=true`, only fields present in grounded GT enter the denominator. Extractor is not penalized for facts never stated in the dialog.
ID exclusion Primary keys and `_id` fields are excluded by default. We evaluate semantic extraction, not synthetic key guessing.
Derived ratio exclusion Fields ending in `_ratio` are excluded. Ratio fields are derived and would otherwise double-count numeric facts.
PII/person-detail exclusions Configured in `03_data_extraction/config/scoring_exclusions.json`. Reduces noise from fields that are not central to financial extraction quality.
4. Train and holdout test logic

Train was used for pipeline development; holdout test is a held-out evaluation

The 316 Train dialogs were used while debugging the extraction pipeline, prompts, scoring, and discrepancy analysis. The 99 holdout test dialogs were generated from households that did not overlap with the Train extracted households and were not used for extractor/evaluator tuning.

Split summary

Split Dialogs Grounded GT pairs Median income P10/P90 income Median assets P10/P90 assets Role
Train 316 316 $132,125 $78,300 / $226,850 $826,600 $389,600 / $1,444,400 Pipeline debugging and tuning
Holdout test 99 99 $121,900 $71,450 / $221,050 $821,650 $431,700 / $1,810,800 Held-out evaluation
5. Results

Train and holdout test performance are closely aligned

Holdout test accuracy is nearly identical to Train at the aggregate field level. The final extraction metrics include the evaluated asset rescue overwrite improvement, with before/after deltas written by the Docker pipeline.

Overall metrics

Dialogs with at least 95% correct fields 297 / 316 = 94.0% 96 / 99 = 97.0%
Dialogs with at least 90% correct fields 315 / 316 = 99.7% 99 / 99 = 100.0%
Missing extracted cells 73 33
Invented extra cells 0 0
Value mismatch cells 176 45

6. Quality controls

Each stage has its own checks before the next stage consumes its outputs

Quality is controlled as a pipeline property, not only as a final accuracy number. We validate the generated household data, the dialog generation process, the dialog-grounded GT construction, extraction behavior on Train, and the full pipeline on the holdout test.

1. Synthetic data quality

The household generator is checked with business rules that enforce relationships between fields: lifecycle date constraints, parent/child age consistency, mortgage burden caps, alimony-marital-status compatibility, and future final-payment dates for non-revolving liabilities.

Statistical tests compare the generated data to priors using distributional diagnostics such as PSI and Jensen-Shannon divergence. Anomaly detection is run with two complementary models: an autoencoder reconstruction score and an IsolationForest score.

Rule violations Distance to priors Anomaly scores Autoencoder top anomalies IsolationForest top anomalies

2. Dialog generation quality

Dialogs are generated in pieces rather than as a single monolithic response: persona, outline, phase-by-phase transcript, state updates, and optional final polishing. This reduces drift and makes the conversation easier to validate incrementally.

Evidence artifacts map profile facts to transcript excerpts. The generation pipeline also writes metrics and uses validation checks, including regex-style and normalization-based checks, to catch fields that are not properly grounded in the transcript. A DeepSeek realism judge adds a separate conversational-quality gate.

Dialog QA details Evidence prompt DeepSeek realism prompt Normalization rules

3. Ground truth quality

Extraction scoring uses dialog-grounded GT whenever evidence is available. This is a sparse GT profile built only from fields that have evidence in the transcript, so the evaluator does not ask the extractor to recover unspoken facts from the full synthetic profile.

The GT export reports how many dialogs were seen, how many profiles were written, how many evidence items were used, and whether any dialogs were missing profile metadata or household identifiers. During holdout evaluation, the pipeline confirmed 99 dialogs written, 99 grounded profiles used, and no missing profile/text rows.

Train grounded GT Holdout grounded GT Train pairing summary Holdout pairing summary

4. Extraction quality on Train

The 316-dialog Train set was used to debug the extractor, prompts, normalization, rescue passes, record matching, and error reporting. This is where we iterated on the pipeline and inspected discrepancy tables to identify systematic extraction failures.

Train quality is monitored through extracted status reports, coercion issues, coverage aggregates, field-level discrepancy stats, value-mismatch tables, and accuracy histograms.

Train extraction report Coercion issues Field stats Value mismatches

5. End-to-end pipeline quality on the holdout test

The holdout test validates the complete pipeline after tuning: held-out dialog generation, grounded GT export, extraction, record matching, scoring, discrepancy analysis, and final metrics. The holdout households do not overlap with the 316 Train households used during pipeline development.

This final gate is the closest approximation to an honest out-of-sample test: the same code and thresholds are applied, but the 99 dialogs were not part of extractor/evaluator iteration.

Holdout extraction report Holdout accuracy report Holdout discrepancy summary Holdout discrepancy report Holdout metrics table
7. Next steps

Research and product directions

The current benchmark gives us a controlled end-to-end baseline. The next phase should make the dialogs more realistic, stress the extractor under harder conditions, and connect extraction errors to real product outcomes.

1. Improve dialog realism with multi-model critique

Send generated dialogs to several model families such as Grok, Qwen, DeepSeek, GPT, Gemini, Claude, and others. Ask each model for a concrete action plan for improving realism, aggregate feedback across dialogs and models, and iteratively update the dialog-generation prompts.

Expected effect: More realistic synthetic dialogs should make the benchmark closer to real calls and increase confidence that extraction performance transfers to production conversations.

2. Run noise-based stress tests

Inject controlled noise into dialogs and measure how extraction metrics degrade. Examples include typos, transcript artifacts, repeated turns, missing punctuation, speaker-label noise, irrelevant side conversations, partial truncation, and contradictory corrections.

Expected effect: The extractor should become more robust to imperfect transcripts, and the team can learn which noise patterns require validation, retries, or manual review.

3. Build adversarial dialog attacks

Train or prompt an adversarial model to make minimal dialog edits that break the extractor. Use these attacks to design defenses, stronger validation, or a more robust extraction architecture.

Expected effect: The system should fail less silently on edge cases and become more stable under confusing, contradictory, or intentionally difficult conversations.

4. Compare structured output modes

Compare extraction with and without structured generation / structured output constraints. Measure not only accuracy, but also parse failures, coercion issues, latency, retry rate, and cost.

Expected effect: Better schema adherence should reduce parsing failures, simplify post-processing, and make model behavior more predictable.

5. Study dialog length versus extraction quality

Quantify how transcript length affects extraction quality and latency. Identify the dialog size at which extraction should switch from single-pass processing to batching, chunking, or hierarchical extraction.

Expected effect: The pipeline can choose the cheapest reliable extraction strategy for each dialog length, reducing latency and cost without sacrificing accuracy.

6. Add a pre-enrichment mode

Improve client experience by pre-filling as much information as possible from public or permissioned sources, such as public professional profiles or other available records. The dialog can then focus only on missing, ambiguous, or stale facts, shortening the conversation while still refreshing outdated information.

Expected effect: Calls can become shorter and more focused, while extraction quality may improve because the model has fewer missing or ambiguous facts to recover from speech alone.

7. Make infrastructure failure-tolerant

Design graceful failover for API outages and provider degradation. For example, if GPT is unavailable, switch to Gemini or another provider, but only through a pipeline that has been explicitly tested and calibrated for that fallback model.

Expected effect: Production extraction becomes more reliable during provider outages while keeping quality changes visible and measurable.

8. Evaluate multiple extractor models and ensembles

Test different LLMs as extractors and compare single-model, fallback, voting, and specialist-ensemble setups. Track accuracy, field-specific failure modes, latency, cost, and operational reliability.

Expected effect: The team can choose a model setup based on measured accuracy, cost, and reliability rather than defaulting to a single provider or model family.

9. Add inference-time extraction quality prediction

Train or calibrate a quality estimator that scores each extracted dialog after inference, even when ground truth is unavailable. Use signals such as schema/coercion issues, missing critical fields, low evidence density, internal inconsistency, rescue-pass changes, and model self-check disagreement.

Expected effect: Risky extractions can be routed to human review or targeted re-processing, reducing silent failures in real-world usage.

10. Build durable dialog and artifact storage

Move dialogs, raw model outputs, extracted profiles, evaluation rows, and review decisions into a proper storage layer, such as object storage plus a relational database or a KV/document store for transcript-level artifacts. Keep stable IDs, versions, model metadata, and audit links between dialog, extraction, and review records.

Expected effect: The system becomes easier to audit, replay, compare across model versions, and operate at scale, while storage and retrieval costs can be controlled more deliberately.

11. Run real-world A/B validation

Compare this pipeline against the current production approach on real dialogs. Measure actual error patterns, human review burden, correction rates, and how extraction errors correlate with downstream product metrics.

Expected effect: The benchmark can be grounded in real operational outcomes, making it clearer which extractor improvements matter most for users and reviewers.

8. Suggested and developed improvement

A targeted asset rescue overwrite pass reduced the main remaining error cluster

The discrepancy analysis showed that the strongest residual errors were concentrated in asset ownership, jointness, provider type, asset type, and subtype. The implemented improvement reuses transcript-derived asset rescue output as a narrow correction source, then measures before/after impact on both Train and holdout test artifacts.

Suggested improvement

Add a specialist correction step for assets instead of re-running the whole extraction. The goal is to fix high-confidence asset labels while keeping unrelated household, income, liability, and protection fields stable.

Before the improvement, some asset fields were filled from early or indirect clues in the dialog. If the client later clarified the same asset more precisely, the rescue pass could not overwrite fields that were already populated. For example, an account could remain labeled as `retirement` even after a later clarification made it clear that the same-value asset was actually `cash`.

Developed implementation

The pipeline now scores the original extraction as a baseline, applies `apply_asset_rescue_overwrite.py`, then scores the improved extraction. To avoid correcting the wrong account, the rescue pass updates an asset only when its monetary value exactly matches the value of an asset from the original extraction.

After the improvement, the post-extraction pass compares each baseline asset with transcript-derived rescue assets. If the monetary value is an exact match, it treats the two records as the same real-world asset and overwrites selected fields with the clarified values: `owner`, `is_joint`, `provider_type`, `asset_type`, and `subtype`. No new asset records are invented, and non-asset entities are left unchanged.

No-leak boundary

The improvement step consumes only baseline extracted JSON and asset-rescue JSON produced from the transcript. Ground truth, evidence files, discrepancy tables, and evaluation outputs are used only after extraction is frozen.

Operational result

The improvement is intentionally modest and conservative: it changes 21 Train dialogs and 4 holdout dialogs, improves the primary asset error cluster, and records the metric delta automatically in `improvement_delta.md` and `improvement_delta.json` during `docker compose up --build`.
